content.text: Periodic Compliance Attestations for Claude Programs

Compliance attestations are signed declarations that specific controls were in place and operating effectively during a defined period. They differ from audit reports (which are produced by external auditors) and control evidence (which is the underlying data): an attestation is the organization's own affirmation that it has reviewed the evidence and stands behind its compliance posture.

Enterprise customers, regulators, and procurement teams increasingly require attestations on a defined cadence — quarterly for some financial services controls, annually for most SOC 2 and ISO equivalents. Building a systematic attestation program for Claude integrations avoids the last-minute scramble that produces errors and omissions.
Claude programs generate attestation obligations from three sources:
Management assertion: that the system description is fairly presented and controls were operating effectively
Management review sign-off on ISMS/AIMS performance
Vendor security questionnaire responses; annual compliance certification letters
Acknowledgment of acceptable use policy; no prohibited use certification
EU AI Act deployer obligations; NIST AI RMF alignment self-assessment
AI owner attestation that each Claude use case remains within approved risk appetite
Quarterly or annually per use case

The most operationally important attestation for Claude programs is the AI Owner's quarterly sign-off on each active Claude use case. This creates a chain of accountability: the person responsible for a use case certifies, on the record, that it is operating within its approved risk parameters. Four questions drive the attestation:
Has the use case changed materially since the last attestation (new data sources, new user population, change in system prompt)?
Are all controls identified in the AI Impact Assessment still in place and operating?
Have any incidents occurred since the last attestation that should be recorded in the risk register?
Is the residual risk still within the approved appetite, or does the risk register need updating?

# quarterly_attestation.py — AI Owner quarterly use-case attestation workflow

import json, datetime, hashlib
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class QuarterlyAttestation:
    use_case_id: str
    use_case_name: str
    attestation_period: str        # e.g. "2026-Q2"
    ai_owner: str
    material_change: bool
    all_controls_operating: bool
    incidents_since_last: int
    residual_risk_still_acceptable: bool
    notes: Optional[str] = None
    signed_at: Optional[str] = None
    signature_hash: Optional[str] = None

    def sign(self) -> "QuarterlyAttestation":
        payload = json.dumps({
            "use_case_id": self.use_case_id,
            "period": self.attestation_period,
            "ai_owner": self.ai_owner,
            "signed_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
            "all_controls_operating": self.all_controls_operating,
            "incidents": self.incidents_since_last,
        }, sort_keys=True)
        self.signed_at = datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z"
        self.signature_hash = hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()
        return self

    def is_complete(self) -> bool:
        return self.signature_hash is not None

    def requires_escalation(self) -> bool:
        return (
            self.material_change
            or not self.all_controls_operating
            or not self.residual_risk_still_acceptable
            or self.incidents_since_last > 0
        )

# Example
att = QuarterlyAttestation(
    use_case_id="CS-001",
    use_case_name="Customer Support Answer Drafting",
    attestation_period="2026-Q2",
    ai_owner="jane.doe@example.com",
    material_change=False,
    all_controls_operating=True,
    incidents_since_last=0,
    residual_risk_still_acceptable=True,
).sign()

print(f"Attestation signed: {att.signature_hash[:16]}...")
print(f"Requires escalation: {att.requires_escalation()}")
The SOC 2 management assertion is a signed statement included at the front of the SOC 2 Type II report. Management — typically the CEO, CISO, or both — asserts that:
The system description fairly presents the system that was designed and implemented throughout the audit period
The controls stated in the description were suitably designed to provide reasonable assurance that the trust services criteria would be met if the controls operated as described
The controls operated effectively throughout the period

For Claude deployments, the most legally significant element is 'throughout the period' — this means every control must have evidence of continuous operation, not just spot-checks. Preparing the management assertion requires the compliance team to:
Review all quarterly internal audit reports and verify open findings were closed or risk-accepted
Confirm all control evidence populations are complete for the full audit window
Verify that any scope changes (new Claude use cases, retired integrations) are documented
Obtain sign-off from all control owners that their controls operated as described
Draft the management assertion text in coordination with external auditors
Route the assertion for legal review before signing

The management assertion exposes signatories to legal liability if it is materially false. Treat it with the same rigour as a financial certification — do not sign without completing the evidence review.
Large enterprise customers often require annual compliance letters separate from the SOC 2 report. These letters certify specific points relevant to the customer's own compliance program:
No sub-processors have access to customer data without authorization
Sub-processor list; DPA with each sub-processor including Anthropic
PII was processed only for contracted purposes
Data processing log; DLP scan results
AI outputs were subject to human review for defined use cases
Human oversight logs; override rate metrics
No Claude model version change occurred without customer notification (where contractually required)
Model version change log; notification records
Highly regulated customers with AI change management clauses
Security controls (access management, encryption, key rotation) operated throughout the year
Control evidence from SOC 2 Type II or internal audit

# compliance_letter_generator.py — generates customer compliance attestation draft

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_compliance_letter(
    customer_name: str,
    service_description: str,
    certification_points: list[str],
    evidence_references: dict[str, str],
    signatory_name: str,
    signatory_title: str,
    period: str,
) -> str:
    system = (
        "You are a compliance specialist drafting an annual attestation letter to an enterprise customer. "
        "The letter must: (1) be professional and precise, (2) certify each listed point with reference to "
        "the supporting evidence, (3) include appropriate qualifications ('to the best of our knowledge', "
        "'based on our review'), (4) not make absolute guarantees beyond what the evidence supports. "
        "Format as a formal business letter."
    )
    user = json.dumps({
        "customer": customer_name,
        "service": service_description,
        "attestation_period": period,
        "certification_points": certification_points,
        "evidence_references": evidence_references,
        "signatory": f"{signatory_name}, {signatory_title}",
    })
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=1200,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

# Usage — always route draft through legal before sending
# letter = generate_compliance_letter(
#     customer_name="Acme Financial Corp",
#     service_description="AI-assisted customer support drafting platform",
#     certification_points=[
#         "PII processed only for contracted customer support purposes",
#         "Human review gate active for all AI-drafted responses",
#         "No unauthorized sub-processor access to customer conversation data",
#     ],
#     evidence_references={
#         "PII processing": "SOC 2 Type II 2025 report, CC9.1",
#         "Human review": "Output review logs, Q1–Q4 2025",
#         "Sub-processor": "Anthropic DPA executed 2024-03-15",
#     },
#     signatory_name="Jane Smith",
#     signatory_title="Chief Information Security Officer",
#     period="January 1 – December 31, 2025",
# )
A well-instrumented compliance program produces attestation-ready evidence continuously rather than assembling it reactively. The following pipeline tests key attestation assertions automatically:

# attestation_readiness_check.py
# Runs weekly; confirms key attestation assertions are evidence-backed

import boto3, json, datetime
from collections import defaultdict

s3 = boto3.client("s3")
EVIDENCE_BUCKET = "my-company-compliance-evidence"

def count_evidence_records(control_id: str, months: list[str]) -> int:
    total = 0
    for month in months:
        prefix = f"evidence/{control_id}/{month}/"
        resp = s3.list_objects_v2(Bucket=EVIDENCE_BUCKET, Prefix=prefix)
        total += resp.get("KeyCount", 0)
    return total

def check_attestation_readiness(period_months: list[str]) -> dict:
    REQUIRED_CONTROLS = {
        "CC6.1": "API key rotation",
        "CC8.1": "System prompt change control",
        "CC9.1": "Output content filtering",
        "A1.1":  "Uptime monitoring",
        "PI1.1": "Processing integrity log",
        "C1.1":  "PII scrubbing",
        "P2.1":  "Consent check",
    }

    results = {}
    gaps = []
    for ctrl_id, ctrl_name in REQUIRED_CONTROLS.items():
        count = count_evidence_records(ctrl_id, period_months)
        ready = count > 0
        results[ctrl_id] = {
            "name": ctrl_name,
            "evidence_records": count,
            "attestation_ready": ready,
        }
        if not ready:
            gaps.append(ctrl_id)

    return {
        "period": period_months,
        "checked_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "controls": results,
        "gaps": gaps,
        "overall_ready": len(gaps) == 0,
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Check current quarter
    months = ["2026-04", "2026-05", "2026-06"]
    report = check_attestation_readiness(months)
    print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
    if not report["overall_ready"]:
        print(f"\nATTESTATION GAPS: {report['gaps']}")
        print("Resolve before signing management assertion.")

Attestation deadlines are non-negotiable — regulators and customers have their own reporting cycles that cannot be delayed because your evidence collection ran late. Maintain a master attestation calendar that assigns a preparation start date for each attestation, accounting for the lead time required:
Block the preparation start dates on the compliance team calendar as recurring events. Missing a preparation start date is the leading cause of rushed, inaccurate attestations.

Periodic compliance attestations are the compliance program's binding commitment — signed declarations that controls operated as described and that the organization's AI programs remained within their approved risk parameters. For Claude deployments, the critical attestation types are the AI Owner's quarterly use-case sign-off, the annual SOC 2 management assertion, and customer compliance letters. Each relies on continuous evidence collection rather than retroactive assembly; an evidence gap discovered during attestation preparation cannot be retroactively filled. Build an automated readiness check that runs weekly against the evidence pipeline, so gaps surface months before the attestation deadline rather than days before the signing.
